Embedding Python Code in UltiSnips Snippets

Using Python interpolation to generate dynamic content inside Vim snippet expansions

vim
python
neovim
configuration

I did not really know that UltiSnips could execute Python code at expansion time until I needed a snippet that generated a variable number of elements based on user input.

Author

Ronald ‘Ryy’ G. Thomas

Published

July 20, 2025

When static snippets are not enough, Python interpolation fills the gap.

1 Introduction

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – 2-3 paragraphs. Open with an “I didn’t really know…” hook about UltiSnips Python interpolation. Reference the Stack Overflow question that prompted this post: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/78636197

Describe the problem: needing a snippet that generates a variable number of elements (e.g., music chord placeholders). Transition to what this post will cover.]

1.1 Motivations

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – 4-6 bullet points. Personal and specific reasons. Examples: static snippets were too rigid, needed parametric expansion, wanted to automate repetitive patterns in code, saw the Stack Overflow question and wanted to solve it, etc.]

1.2 Objectives

  1. [YOUR CONTENT HERE – specific, measurable]
  2. [YOUR CONTENT HERE]
  3. [YOUR CONTENT HERE]
  4. [YOUR CONTENT HERE]

I am documenting my learning process here. If you spot errors or have better approaches, please let me know.

Vim’s extensibility through UltiSnips and Python creates a powerful text generation system.

2 Prerequisites and Setup

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – 1-2 paragraphs. What readers need: Vim 8+ compiled with Python3 support, UltiSnips plugin installed (via vim-plug, Vundle, or pathogen). Show how to verify Python support.]

" Check Python support in Vim
:echo has('python3')

" UltiSnips configuration in .vimrc
Plug 'SirVer/ultisnips'
let g:UltiSnipsExpandTrigger = "<tab>"
let g:UltiSnipsJumpForwardTrigger = "<c-j>"
let g:UltiSnipsJumpBackwardTrigger = "<c-k>"

3 What is UltiSnips Python Interpolation?

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – 1-2 paragraphs in plain language. Explain that UltiSnips supports three interpolation types: shell (`), VimScript (!v), and Python (!p). Python interpolation executes Python code at snippet expansion time and inserts the result. Use an analogy – e.g., a regular snippet is a rubber stamp, but Python interpolation is a mail merge that customises each stamp.]

4 Getting Started: Basic Python Snippets

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – First code examples. Start with simple Python interpolation: current date, simple string manipulation. Follow CODE -> OUTPUT -> INTERPRETATION. Show the snippet definition and what it expands to.]

# [YOUR SNIPPET HERE -- basic Python example]
snippet today "Insert today's date"
`!p snip.rv = __import__('datetime').date.today().isoformat()`
endsnippet

[YOUR INTERPRETATION HERE – 2-4 sentences explaining the syntax: !p marker, snip.rv, Python imports.]

# [YOUR SNIPPET HERE -- second basic example]

[YOUR INTERPRETATION HERE]

Learning the interpolation syntax opens up a wide range of dynamic snippet possibilities.

4.1 The Chord Generator: Solving the Stack Overflow

Problem

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – The core section. Show the original Stack Overflow problem: generating a variable number of chord placeholders. Present the Python function from the original post:]

def make_chords(num_reps):
    text = ""
    for i in range(num_reps):
        text += "< > "
    return text

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – Show how to embed this in an UltiSnips snippet definition. Walk through the syntax step by step.]

# [YOUR SNIPPET HERE -- chord generator]

[YOUR INTERPRETATION HERE]

Key resources:

  • UltiSnips Documentation – Official UltiSnips reference
  • UltiSnips Python Interpolation (Vimcasts) – Video tutorial on Python snippets
  • Stack Overflow: Python in UltiSnips – The question that prompted this post
  • LuaSnip (Neovim alternative) – Lua-based snippet engine for Neovim
  • Vim Python Interface Documentation – Official Vim-Python integration reference

" To verify your setup:
:echo has('python3')

I would enjoy hearing from you if:

  • You spot an error or a better approach to any of the code in this post.
  • You have suggestions for topics you would like to see covered.
  • You want to discuss R programming, data science, or reproducible research.
  • You have questions about anything in this tutorial.
  • You just want to say hello and connect.
